data.title: Thanksgiving dinner cost declines for third straight year: AFBF survey
data.description:
The cost of a Thanksgiving dinner will be lower this year, marking the third consecutive annual decline in holiday meal costs, according to the American Farm Bureau Federation.

The AFBF estimated the cost of a holiday meal for 10 this year would be $55.18 (or about $5.52 per person), down 5% from 2024.

The price of a Thanksgiving turkey saw a steep drop this year, helping pull overall meal costs lower. A 16-pound frozen bird now averages $21.50, or $1.34 per pound - a decline of more than 16% from a year ago.

The estimates are based on a survey by AFBF volunteer shoppers, who checked the prices of items used in a typical Thanksgiving dinner in the first week of November.

The bureau’s survey menu has remained unchanged since it began conducting the yearly informal survey in 1986 to allow for consistent price comparisons. It has 12 items, including stuffing mix, pie crusts, dinner rolls, sweet potatoes, and cranberries.

“While the wholesale price for fresh turkey is up from 2024, grocery stores are featuring Thanksgiving deals and attempting to draw consumer demand back to turkey, leading to lower retail prices for a holiday bird,” the AFBF said in a statement.

The bureau noted that the average per-pound feature price for whole frozen turkeys dropped further in the second week of November, citing USDA Agricultural Marketing Service data.

However, despite three consecutive years of declining costs, the price of a Thanksgiving dinner still hasn’t fully eased from a sharp jump in 2022 to a record $64.05, according to the AFBF.

Food prices remain a point of concern, especially heading into Thanksgiving and a record-long government shutdown delaying economic data. The most recent CPI report, from September, showed inflation running at 3%, the highest since January. Many household staples saw their biggest monthly increases in three years, with more than half the items in the index rising more than 3%.

Major publicly listed retailers central to U.S. Thanksgiving dinner shopping include Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), Kroger (KR), Albertsons (ACI), Costco (COST), BJ’s Wholesale (BJ), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), and Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM). Key turkey and holiday-food suppliers include Hormel (HRL), Tyson (TSN), and Pilgrim’s Pride (PPC).

data.title: Michael Burry Says He’s Still Trading — Bloomberg
data.description:
Michael Burry says he remains active in markets even as Scion Asset Management relinquishes its registration with the Securities and Exchange Commission, Bloomberg reported.

In an email to Bloomberg News, Burry said the most recent iteration of Scion operated as “essentially a friends and family fund” and was never intended to gather outside capital.

“I didn’t market it or treat it like most do,” he said. “I am still running my money and active in markets.”

Burry formally ended Scion’s RIA status earlier this month and hinted on X that he was “on to much better things Nov. 25.” He emphasized that Scion Asset Management is not shutting down, but will no longer manage money for external investors.

The 54-year-old investor — best known for his winning bet against subprime mortgages before the 2008 financial crisis — continues to draw attention for his market commentary and regulatory filings. Recent disclosures showed bearish positions in Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR), along with call options tied to Halliburton (HAL) and Pfizer (PFE).

Burry added he welcomed being “free of the compliance burden and broad misunderstandings generated by my filings.”

data.title: British Land Company reports 1H results
data.description:
• British Land Company press release (BTLCY): 1H GAAP EPS of 15.4p, up 1% (HY25: 15.3p)
  • Underlying Profit £155m, up 8% (HY25: £143m)
  • £59m of assets disposed at an average of 5% above book value
  • £52m of retail acquired, principally two retail parks, at 8.4% Topped Up NIY
  • OUTLOOK: Like-for-like net rental growth expected to be c.5% for FY26; Reiterating guidance of 3-5% per annum ERV growth across the portfolio; Expect FY26 Underlying EPS of at least 28.5p, with growth of at least 6% expected for FY27 (30.2p), and 3-6% beyond; Comfortable with current market expectations for Underlying EP

data.title: More U.S. homes are losing value, but it's not a crash - Zillow
data.description:
Home values have declined for more than half of U.S. households in the past year, according to Zillow (Z), marking the highest share since the end of the 2000s housing crisis.

Zillow (Z) estimates that 53% of homes lost value over the past year as of October, up from 14% a year ago. That's the highest share of homes declining in value since April 2012, when the housing crash was starting to bottom out.

"Home values surged over the past six years, and the vast majority of homeowners still have significant equity," said Treh Manhertz, senior economic researcher, Zillow (Z). "What we're seeing now is a normalization, not a crash."

The vast majority of U.S. homeowners have seen their home values rise substantially in the time they've owned them - 67% growth at the median.

Manhertz said relatively few homeowners are selling at a loss - 4.1% of homes have lost value since their last sale, a smaller share than before the pandemic. 

Notably, loss of value over the past year has been most widespread in the West and South, while the losses have largely missed the Northeast and Midwest so far.

data.title: Euro Area inflation drops to 2.1% in October
data.description:
• The Eurozone annual inflation rate held at 2.1% in October 2025, down slightly from 2.2% in September, staying close to the European Central Bank’s 2% target. The final figures meet broader market consensus.

  • Food, alcohol, and tobacco prices rose more slowly at 2.5%, compared with 3.0% the previous month, driven by slower gains in both processed food and unprocessed food.

  • Among the largest Eurozone economies, inflation slowed in Germany, Italy, and France, but Spain saw a rise.

  • The Consumer Price Index increased 0.2% M/M in October, after a 0.1% rise in September, matching the first estimate. 

  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX)
  • Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Ocado beaten down after Kroger's plan to close automated warehouses
data.description:
Online grocery retailer in the UK—Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF)—fell sharply, about 17% on Tuesday on the London Stock Exchange after U.S. grocery retailer Kroger (KR) said that it will close three of its automated warehouses that were built in partnership with Ocado (OTCPK:OCDGF).

The closures are set for January and represent a significant setback to Ocado's (OTCPK:OCDGF) growth story in the U.S. market, which is its most important foreign market partnership.

Kroger (KR) cited these sites had not met financial expectations and plans instead to shift its e-commerce strategy away from capital-intensive automated warehouses toward expanding grocery delivery through partnerships with delivery firms like Instacart (CART), DoorDash (DASH), and Uber Eats (UBER).

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: PLUG, ON, and more
data.description:
Stock futures showed a cautious tone in early Wednesday trading as investors awaited Nvidia’s highly anticipated third-quarter earnings, scheduled for release after the market close. 

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • La-Z-Boy (LZB) +7% - Shares jumped after delivering an FQ2 beat, with new store openings and the closure of weaker locations helping lift sales even in what management called a “choppy landscape.” The company also announced a 10% dividend hike, its fifth straight double-digit increase, raising the payout to $0.242 per share. For the current quarter, LZB expects revenue of $525M–$545M (up 1%–4% and bracketing the $534.2M consensus) and guides adjusted operating margin to 5.0%–6.5% vs. 7.1% in the latest quarter.
  • On Semiconductor (ON) +3% - Shares gained after unveiling a three-year, $6B share repurchase program starting January 1, 2026, replacing the current plan set to end on December 31. The company has already bought back $2.1B of stock over the past three years and said the larger authorization underscores its focus on disciplined capital allocation and long-term value creation. ON noted it expects to dedicate nearly all of its 2025 free cash flow to buybacks. The announcement arrived alongside a disclosure of up to $300M in restructuring-related costs tied to previously announced initiatives.

Biggest stock losers

  • Plug Power (PLUG) -15% - Shares tumbled after pricing $375M of 6.75% convertible senior notes due 2033 in a private offering, with an additional $56.25M available to initial purchasers under a 13-day option. The note sale, expected to close on Nov. 21, 2025, will generate approximately $347.2M in net proceeds (or ~$399.4M if the option is fully exercised), as the notes were priced at 95% of face value. Plug Power plans to use roughly $245.6M of the proceeds to fully repay its outstanding principal balance.
  • Eos Energy (EOSE) -2.7% - Shares fell after launching a registered direct common-stock offering to a select group of investors, alongside a private offering of $500M in convertible senior notes due 2031 with an additional $75M option for initial purchasers. The company expects to use proceeds from both offerings to partially repurchase its existing 6.75% convertible senior notes due 2030 and for general corporate purposes, with plans to negotiate private transactions for those repurchases. A concurrent registered direct equity offering is also planned, contingent on the completion of the note issuance.

data.title: Google DeepMind to open AI research lab in Singapore
data.description:
Google DeepMind said it is opening an AI research lab in Singapore.

The Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit noted that the lab will collaborate with government, businesses, civil society, and academic institutions across the Asia-Pacific region.

The team in Singapore will consist of research scientists, software engineers, and AI impact experts focused on critical areas of research and development. 

"This builds on our efforts to pioneer foundational research in linguistic and cultural inclusivity for Asia Pacific, advance Gemini's core capabilities, and apply the latest models across Google products and for Cloud customers," said Google DeepMind's Chief Operating Officer Lila Ibrahim.

Google noted that this investment builds on its commitment to the Asia-Pacific ecosystem, and follows a more-than doubling of Google DeepMind's APAC team over the past year.

data.title: Dutch government suspends powers over Chinese-owned chipmaker Nexperia
data.description:
The Dutch government has suspended its powers over chipmaker Nexperia, restoring control to its Chinese parent company and easing a dispute with Beijing that had begun disrupting global automotive production.

Economic Affairs Minister Vincent Karremans said the decision to withdraw the order — which allowed the Netherlands to block or amend Nexperia’s decisions — was made “as a show of goodwill,” according to a statement posted Wednesday on X.

The dispute began in September when Karremans used a Cold War-era law to assert control over Nexperia, owned by China’s Wingtech. The Dutch Ministry of Economic Affairs said it invoked the Goods Availability Act "due to serious governance shortcomings at semiconductor manufacturer Nexperia."

Beijing hit back with export curbs on components from Nexperia’s Guangdong site, which assembles chips from wafers made in Europe.

The Dutch reversal followed a breakthrough in negotiations involving Chinese and Dutch officials, with support from Germany, the EU, and the US. Beijing agreed to ease export curbs from Nexperia’s Chinese plant, helping to resolve the matter. A Dutch economic affairs delegation traveled to Beijing this week to finalize a “mutually agreeable solution,” the ministry said.

data.title: Sage Non-GAAP EPS of 43.20p, revenue of £2.51B; announces £300m share buyback program, gives FY outlook
data.description:
• Sage Group press release (SGGEF): FY Non-GAAP EPS of 43.20p.
  • Revenue of £2.51B (+7.7% Y/Y).
  • Underlying total revenue increased by 10% to £2,513m, reflecting our high-quality subscription-based recurring revenue model.
  • Underlying annualised recurring revenue (ARR) up 11% to £2,574m, with growth across all regions balanced between new and existing customers.
  • Underlying EBITDA increased by 15% to £694m, with margin increasing by 120 basis points to 27.6%.
  • Renewal rate by value of 101% (FY24: 101%), reflecting strong retention rates and a good level of sales to existing customers.
  • Sage Business Cloud revenue increased by 13% to £2,083m (FY24: £1,837m), including cloud native revenue growth of 23% to £885m (FY24: £718m).
  • Subscription penetration increased to 83% (FY24: 82%) driven by growth in subscription revenue of 12% to £2,093m (FY24: £1,876m).
  • Proposed final dividend of 14.4p, increasing the full year dividend by 7% to 21.85p, in line with our progressive policy.
  • Share buyback program of up to £300m announced separately today, reflecting Sage’s strong cash generation, robust financial position, and the Board’s confidence in Sage’s future prospects.
  • Outlook In FY26, we expect organic total revenue growth to be 9% or above. Operating margins are expected to continue trending upwards in FY26 and beyond, as we focus on efficiently scaling the Group.
